item.title: Clarke questions Mason on whether she disclosed she had issues in NZ when taking up COI job
item.description: Former Deputy Prime Minister, Manoa Kamikamica’s lawyer, Wylie Clarke called out FICAC's first witness, Janet Mason's credibility as he says the Commission of Inquiry expects candour and full disclosures from those that they question but yet Mason did not do the same. During the cross-examination, Clarke referred to Mason's case in New Zealand, asking her before she accepted her apointment as coun
item.content:encoded:
Former Deputy Prime Minister, Manoa Kamikamica’s lawyer, Wylie Clarke called out FICAC's first witness, Janet Mason's credibility as he says the Commission of Inquiry expects candour and full disclosures from those that they question but yet Mason did not do the same.

During the cross-examination, Clarke referred to Mason's case in New Zealand, asking her before she accepted her apointment as counsel assisting with the COI if she had disclosed to the President that she had some issues, to which she said she did not.

Mason however said that she disclosed them to Prime Minister, Sitiveni Rabuka.

While under cross examination by Clarke, Mason was asked if Kamikamica did not know, the Commission of Inquiry did not know, how was Kamikamica supposed to know that he had to offer up information that would be relevant.

Mason says they were trying to find out if Kamikamica knew what the complaint about him with FICAC was about.

Mason says that became apparent when they received the update.

She also told Clarke that the witnesses are under obligation to disclose anything that is relevant.

Clarke then asked how would Kamikamica know what is relevant or not if he does not know what he is being asked about, to which Mason says the COI knew that there were complaints, and Kamikamica was on the list as well as some other Members of Parliament.

Mason then went on to say that the Supervisor of Elections had released a list of people with complaints against them, however, the COI did not know what the complaints were about.

When questioned on whether the COI asked the Judicial Services Commission or the President's Office or even Lands Minister Filimoni Vosarogo who an unnamed source had told Frances Puleiwai that Barbara Malimali being appointed as FICAC Commissioner was being discussed in Cabinet, Mason said they did not.

The trial will continue today where FICAC's second witness, Government MP Kalaveti Ravu will take the stand.

Kamikamica is charged by FICAC for perjury and giving false information to a public servant.

item.title: Children with disabilities continue to face barriers to education
item.description: Many children with disabilities continue to struggle to access education because of transport difficulties, inaccessible school environments, discrimination, high support needs, a lack of specialist support, limited access to special schools outside urban areas and unstable home environments. This has been highlighted in the Rights of Children with Disabilities Report.
item.content:encoded:
Many children with disabilities continue to struggle to access education because of transport difficulties, inaccessible school environments, discrimination, high support needs, a lack of specialist support, limited access to special schools outside urban areas and unstable home environments.

This has been highlighted in the Rights of Children with Disabilities Report.

The report says more students with disabilities are now enrolled in mainstream schools than in special schools, with 1,867 attending mainstream schools compared with 1,285 in special schools.

This highlights the growing impact of efforts to promote inclusive education in Fiji.

The report highlights that there have been important advances in inclusive education, including the implementation of the Special and Inclusive Education Policy, grants for accommodations and assistive devices, and ongoing teacher training initiatives.

Parents told researchers that their children enjoy attending school, have a strong desire to learn, and deserve the same right to education as every other child, stressing that school helps children develop valuable social skills and a sense of belonging.

According to the report, there are 326 mainstream primary schools and 139 mainstream secondary schools in Fiji that enrol students with disabilities.

Students with disabilities have also been supported through measures such as enlarged-print learning materials, modified lessons, sign language interpreters, and teacher aide support.

Some schools have improved accessibility by building ramps and railings, providing transport for students, and introducing inclusive classroom technology.

Despite these improvements, the report says children with higher support needs continue to face the greatest challenges, with some never attending school, starting school late, leaving early, or missing out on vocational education opportunities altogether.

It also reveals a significant gender gap, with almost twice as many boys with disabilities enrolled in both mainstream and special schools as girls, unlike the general student population, where enrolment is almost evenly split between boys and girls.

While many teachers have been praised for supporting children with disabilities, the report says others have displayed discriminatory attitudes or focused more on high-achieving students without disabilities.

It says that while 115 teachers have specialist qualifications in special education, many have not received the training needed to adequately support children with disabilities.

The report also raises concerns that some mainstream schools are encouraging parents to enrol their children in special schools because they believe they are unable to provide the necessary support.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: New boat gives Rewa fisherman chance to grow business
item.description: Chand and his family from Newtown Settlement in Lokia received the assistance under the Government's Seafarer Entrepreneurship Assistance (SEA) Programme. Minister for Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management Mosese Bulitavu handed over the boat and engine, saying the assistance would help the family grow its fishing business.
item.content:encoded:
Chand and his family from Newtown Settlement in Lokia received the assistance under the Government's Seafarer Entrepreneurship Assistance (SEA) Programme.

Minister for Rural and Maritime Development and Disaster Management Mosese Bulitavu handed over the boat and engine, saying the assistance would help the family grow its fishing business.

Bulitavu says the larger and safer boat will allow Chand to fish more efficiently, increase his catch and earn a better income for his family.

He says the SEA Programme is helping rural and maritime families improve their livelihoods by supporting fishing and other marine-based businesses.

Under the programme, recipients contribute one-third of the total cost, while the Government funds the remaining two-thirds.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Young families celebrate dream of home ownership through Housing Authority scheme
item.description: Qalaivecena says receiving the key to their new home is a special feeling because, unlike renting, they will now own the house they are living in. He says paying rent is very different, and as a newly married couple, owning their own house gives them the opportunity to build their future and raise a family.
item.content:encoded:
Qalaivecena says receiving the key to their new home is a special feeling because, unlike renting, they will now own the house they are living in.

He says paying rent is very different, and as a newly married couple, owning their own house gives them the opportunity to build their future and raise a family.

Qalaivecena is also encouraging young people not to be discouraged from applying for housing assistance, saying these opportunities can make a real difference in helping them achieve home ownership.

Meanwhile, 24-year-old Shaunil Chand says owning a home at a young age is something he never imagined would have been possible without the Government's support.

Chand says he is grateful to the Housing Authority and the Government for the housing scheme and grant, which have made home ownership more affordable for young people like him.

He also thanked his parents and family for encouraging and supporting him throughout the process.

Chand is encouraging other young people to apply for the Housing Authority's housing schemes.

Insert: Chand encouraging young people 21th July 26

31 successful applicants having now received keys under the Nepani Model Homes Project, while 70 more applications remain under assessment.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Ratu Sukuna and Extension Street road upgrade to deliver safer roads and smoother traffic
item.description: Meanwhile, China Railway No. 5 Group General Manager Jin Ming says the company is honoured to be entrusted with delivering the projects and values its long-standing relationship with the Fiji Roads Authority. He says the launch marks another milestone in the shared commitment to improving Fiji's infrastructure, strengthening connectivity and supporting the nation's sustainable development.
item.content:encoded:
Meanwhile, China Railway No. 5 Group General Manager Jin Ming says the company is honoured to be entrusted with delivering the projects and values its long-standing relationship with the Fiji Roads Authority.

He says the launch marks another milestone in the shared commitment to improving Fiji's infrastructure, strengthening connectivity and supporting the nation's sustainable development.

He says the Ratu Sukuna Road Upgrade has a contract period of 364 days, and while the project is relatively short in length, it presents significant engineering and management challenges, particularly with relation to the relocation and coordination of underground utility services.

The General Manager says Ratu Sukuna Road is a busy urban transport corridor, meaning construction will take place in a constrained environment with heavy traffic and nearby residential communities.

He says careful traffic management, construction planning and communication with the public will be essential throughout the project.

He adds that the Extension Street Road Link project is less than one kilometre long and is expected to be completed within about four months.

The General Manager says the company has mobilised an experienced project management team, engineers, skilled workers, equipment and technical resources to ensure the projects are delivered safely, on schedule and to a high standard.

He says the company will work closely with the Fiji Roads Authority, consultants, utility providers, local communities and other stakeholders to minimise disruption to the public.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Police confirm that man found dead in Nausori died of natural causes
item.description: Police have confirmed that the man whose body was found in Nausori last Friday morning died of natural causes. A post-mortem examination was conducted to determine the cause of death.
item.content:encoded:
Police have confirmed that the man whose body was found in Nausori last Friday morning died of natural causes.

A post-mortem examination was conducted to determine the cause of death.

The deceased was a resident of Vuci Road.

item.title: Police should swiftly arrest those making threats against Fiji's peace - Turaga
item.description: Acting Attorney General Siromi Turaga says if there are people making inflammatory comments against the peace of this country, the
